title: INFO 526 Final Project Proposal
text:
TidyTuesday | Glottolog Github
There are three primary datasets from TidyTuesday, each of which are derived from Glottolog, which is an open-access linguistics database.
The focus of the data are the languages spoken across the world. Primarily where they are spoken (both in terms of macroarea and lat/lon coordinates), and which families they belong to. Beyond that, and maybe the most pointed data, are the endangered statuses of these languages, based on a six-point scale called the Agglomerated Endangerment Scale (AES), which is as follows:









AES status
AES Status Code
# of languages
% of languages




not endangered
1
2770
35.34%


threatened
2
1565
19.96%


shifting
3
1799
22.95%


moribund
4
421
5.37%


nearly extinct
5
300
3.83%


extinct
6
984
12.55%


total:

7839







World Bank Group Data

File name: GDP_by_country_time.csv

Global Gross Domestic Product (GDP) change, year over year (since 1960) | Source: World Bank Group


UN Data

File name: un_developing_countries_data.csv

List of Least Developed Countries (LDCs) | Source: United Nations
List of Landlocked Developing Countries (LLDCs) | Source: United Nations
List of Small Island Developing States (SIDs) | Source: United Nations

File name: unpopulation_dataportal.csv

Population data by country since 1960 for two variables | Source: United Nations

Population density
Populating change







# Gross domestic product, reformatted
gdp <- 
  readr::read_csv("data/GDP_by_country_time.csv", skip = 4) |>
  select(-`Indicator Name`, -`Indicator Code`) |>
  rename(country = `Country Name`, country_code = `Country Code`) |>
  select(where(~!all(is.na(.)))) |>  
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -c(country, country_code),
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "gdp_growth_yoy"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    year = as.integer(year),
    gdp_growth_yoy = as.numeric(gdp_growth_yoy)
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(gdp_growth_yoy))

# United Nations' developing countries list
dev_countries <- 
  readr::read_csv("data/un_developing_countries_data.csv")

# Population change and density data by country
pop <- 
  readr::read_csv("data/unpopulation_dataportal.csv")

title: INFO 526 Final Project Proposal
text:
Analysis Details
I was a linguistics major in undergrad, so languages are naturally interesting to me, but I know very little about how languages intersect with geography. To that end, I’m hoping to do some exploratory analysis alongside of the set questions listed here.
‘Patterns’ could be interpreted many ways, so while I want to keep an open mind, here area at least two questions I want to explore.

Question 1 - Global Patterns

What geographic patterns exist for endangered languages around the world? Specifically:

What relationship exists between a language’s isolate status and its endangered status?
Which language families span the widest geographic range?



Data Wrangling

Create master_languages dataframe

left_join endangered_status on (language) id

status_code
status_label

left_join families on languages$family_id and families$id




Variables











#
Variable
Type
Source
Notes
Used For?




1
Language
Categorical
languages
Individual language name
Both


2
Macroarea
Categorical
languages
Africa, Americas, Australia, Eurasia, Pacific, South America
Both


3
Country
Categorical
languages
Join key for supplementary datasets
Both


4
Latitude / Longitude
Numerical
languages
Geographic coordinates for map placement
Both


5
Family ID
Categorical
languages
Links to families dataset
Part 2


6
Is Isolate?
Categorical (Boolean)
languages
True if language belongs to no known family
Part 1


7
Endangered Status Label
Categorical (Ordinal)
endangered_status
not endangered → threatened → shifting → moribund → nearly extinct → extinct
Part 1


8
Endangered Status Code
Numerical (Ordinal)
endangered_status
AES scale 1–6
Part 1


9
Landlocked Developing Country (LDC) Designation
Categorical (Boolean)
un_developing_countries_data
UN classification; joined at country level
Part 1


10
Small Island Developing Country (SID) Designation
Categorical (Boolean)
un_developing_countries_data
UN classification; joined at country level
Part 1


11
Global Reach
Numerical
languages
Custom calculated metric1
Part 2





Visualization
The obvious choice here is a world map, but the reach goal is to create a tabular visualization of the same dataset that allows for different perspectives of the same data. I think this is especially useful when describing/surveying patterns in data. I expect to product something akin to this, but I’m not married to table, line, and bar charts as the alternative views:



Expectations

Isolate languages will be more likely to be endangered than non-isolates.
Isolate languages will be more commonly found on island countries versus land-locked countries.

Supplementary Data: un_developing_countries_data.csv

Child languages of English and Spanish will have the widest global reach.



Limitations
I think the key challenge here is my lack of experience with geographic visualizations. This is largely why I chose the topic in the first place, though, and ‘Visualizing geospatial data’ lectures are timely. Beyond that, cleanly defining ‘global reach’ and ‘land-locked countries’ will be important.



Question 2 - Shifting Socioeconomically vs. Shifting Linguistically

What relationship exists between shifting/endangered languages and:

Population change over the last 20 years
Change in GDP over the last 20 years


Endangered Status is qualitative by nature but also includes a numeric scale. Rather than create custom metrics using the AES scale, I’m going to lean on supplementary data (italicized below) for my quantitative analysis.

Data Wrangling

Create master_countries dataframe

Begin with master_languages dataframe
Filter for shifting, moribund, and endangered AES statuses
Filter for years >= 2005 for GDP and Population data




Variables











#
Variable
Type
Source
Notes
Used For?




1
Language
Categorical
languages
Individual language name
Both


3
Country
Categorical
languages
Join key for supplementary datasets
Both


4
Latitude / Longitude
Numerical
languages
Geographic coordinates for map placement
Both


5
Family ID
Categorical
languages
Links to families dataset
Both


7
Endangered Status Label
Categorical (Ordinal)
endangered_status
not endangered → threatened → shifting → moribund → nearly extinct → extinct
Both


8
Endangered Status Code
Numerical (Ordinal)
endangered_status
AES scale 1–6
Both


9
Population change over the last 20 years
Numerical
unpopulation_dataportal
Year-over-year population change by country; joined at country level
Part 2


10
Change in GDP over the last 20 years
Numerical
GDP_by_country_time
Year-over-year GDP change by country; joined at country level
Part 2





Visualization
Even though I don’t have endangered status data over time (see: limitations), I still plan to incorporate time series/trend visuals for change in GDP and population. The goal is combine change over time and categorical data (endangered status today) in a way that’s equally meaningful and intuitive.


Expectations

The highest concentration of shifting languages will be in countries/areas that have seen the largest decline in population over the last 20 years.
shifting, moribund, and endangered languages will roughly follow Pareto’s rule in relation to GDP. That is, roughly 80% of the world’s least spoken languages will exist in countries/areas that make up less than 20% of the world’s GDP.



Limitations
As mentioned, I wasn’t able to find past/trending endangered status data, only the current statuses for languages. This would have been especially useful to show trending over time via time lapse or otherwise. As a solution, I’m going to focus on the last two decades (2005-2025) as a measure of how much a country/macroarea is changing in population and GDP. Then I plan to use that data to compare against the static AES values of shifting, moribund, and endangered.



Project Timeline









Week
Deliverable
Deadline
Status




Week 1
Proposal + Peer Reviews
June 17, 2026
Complete


Week 2
Data Wrangling + Mutation
June 25, 2026
In Progress


Week 3
Visualizations + Begin Writeup
July 1, 2026
In Progress


Week 4
Complete Writeup + Record Presentation
July 3, 2026
In Progress

title: Project title
text:
Math Expressions
You can write LaTeX math expressions inside a pair of dollar signs, e.g. $\alpha+\beta$ renders \(\alpha + \beta\). You can use the display style with double dollar signs:
$$\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i$$
\[
\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i
\]
Limitations:

The source code of a LaTeX math expression must be in one line, unless it is inside a pair of double dollar signs, in which case the starting $$ must appear in the very beginning of a line, followed immediately by a non-space character, and the ending $$ must be at the end of a line, led by a non-space character;
There should not be spaces after the opening $ or before the closing $.

title: Project title
text:
Feeling adventurous?

You are welcomed to use the default styling of the slides. In fact, that’s what I expect majority of you will do. You will differentiate yourself with the content of your presentation.
But some of you might want to play around with slide styling. Some solutions for this can be found at https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/revealjs.

title: The Languages of the World
text:
Part 1 - Endangered vs. Isolate Languages
What relationship exists between a language’s isolate status and its endangered status?

What is a language isolate?
Per Wikipedia, a language isolate, sometimes called an isolated language, is a language that has no demonstrable genealogical relationship with any other language. That is, an isolate is a language family that only contains one member.


How are we defining ‘endangered’ languages?
The endangered statuses of languages, based on a six-point scale called the Agglomerated Endangerment Scale (AES), comes from GlottoScope, which is a collection of global linguists dedicated to tracking endangered languages. The endangered statuses are as follows:









AES status
AES Status Code
# of languages
% of languages




Not Endangered
1
2770
35.34%


Threatened
2
1565
19.96%


Shifting
3
1799
22.95%


Moribund
4
421
5.37%


Nearly Extinct
5
300
3.83%


Extinct
6
984
12.55%


total:

7839






Data Wrangling

Load Primary Data
There are three primary datasets from TidyTuesday, each of which are derived from Glottolog, which is an open-access linguistics database.
The focus of the data are the languages spoken across the world. Primarily where they are spoken (both in terms of macroarea and lat/lon coordinates), and which families they belong to.

# Download raw data and filter to endangered status
endangered_status <- 
  readr::read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/glottolog/glottolog-cldf/refs/heads/master/cldf/values.csv") |> 
  dplyr::filter(Parameter_ID == "aes") |> 
  dplyr::select(Language_ID, Value, Code_ID) |> 
  dplyr::rename(id = Language_ID,
                status_code = Value,
                status_label = Code_ID) |> 
  dplyr::mutate(status_label = stringr::str_replace(stringr::str_remove(status_label, "^aes-"), "_", " "))

# Download language and family data
fam_lgs <- 
  readr::read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/glottolog/glottolog-cldf/refs/heads/master/cldf/languages.csv")

# Filter and clean language family data
families <- 
  fam_lgs |> 
  dplyr::filter(Level == "family") |> 
  dplyr::select(ID, Name) |> 
  dplyr::rename(Family = Name) |> 
  dplyr::rename_with(stringr::str_to_lower, dplyr::everything())

# Filter and clean language data
languages <- 
  fam_lgs |> 
  dplyr::filter(Level == "language") |> 
  dplyr::select(ID, Name, Macroarea, Latitude, Longitude, ISO639P3code, Countries, Is_Isolate, Family_ID) |> 
  dplyr::rename_with(stringr::str_to_lower, dplyr::everything())



Prepare Data

# Create master languages data frame
master_languages <- left_join(
  languages,
  endangered_status,
  by = "id"
) |>
  left_join(
    families,
    by     = c("family_id" = "id"),
    suffix = c("", "_name")
  ) |>
  filter(
    if_all(c(
      latitude, longitude, countries, status_code, status_label
      ), ~ !is.na(.x))
    ) |>
  mutate(
    status_label = str_to_title(status_label),
    status_label = factor(status_label, levels = c(
    "Not Endangered", "Threatened", "Shifting", 
    "Moribund", "Nearly Extinct", "Extinct")),
    not_endangered = if_else(
      status_label == "Not Endangered", "Not Endangered", "Threatened or Worse")
    )




Visualization

ggplot(master_languages, aes(
  x = status_label, 
  fill = is_isolate
  )) + 
  geom_bar(position = "fill") + 
  labs(
    title    = "Proportion of Isolate Languages by Endangered Status",
    subtitle = "Stacked to 100%",
    x        = "Endangered Status Designation",
    y        = "Proportion of Languages",
    fill     = "Is Isolate",
    caption  = "Source: "
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 15),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    plot.caption  = element_text(hjust = 0)
  )










Analysis & Discussion

title: The Languages of the World
text:
Part 2 - Largest Geographic Reach
Which language families span the largest geographic range?
Answering this question is only possible with a functional definition of ‘largest geographic range’. I considered two different options:

Haversine Distance, or essentially a point A to point B line between the two farthest apart speakers
Bounding Box Area, which takes the min and max of latitude and longitude of speakers and creates a rectangle/area


Data Wrangling

Calculate Custom Global Reach Metrics

# --- Option 1: Max Pairwise Haversine Distance ---
global_reach_haversine <- function(lons, lats) {
  coords <- cbind(lons, lats)
  if (nrow(coords) < 2) return(NA_real_)
  
  # distm returns a matrix of pairwise distances in meters
  dist_matrix <- geosphere::distm(coords, fun = distHaversine)
  max(dist_matrix, na.rm = TRUE) / 1000  # return in km
}

# --- Option 2: Bounding Box Area ---
global_reach_bbox <- function(lons, lats) {
  lat_range <- diff(range(lats, na.rm = TRUE))
  lon_range <- diff(range(lons, na.rm = TRUE))
  lat_range * lon_range  # in decimal degrees²
}


family_reach <- master_languages |>
  mutate(family = replace_na(family, "Isolate")) |>
  group_by(family) |>
  filter(!family %in% c(
  "Unattested", "Bookkeeping", "Unclassifiable",
  "Speech Register", "Pidgin", "Mixed Language"
  # "Artificial Language", "Sign Language", "Isolate"
  )) |>
  summarise(
    reach_km         = global_reach_haversine(longitude, latitude),
    reach_bbox_deg2  = global_reach_bbox(longitude, latitude),
    min_lat          = min(latitude, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_lat          = max(latitude, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_lon          = min(longitude, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_lon          = max(longitude, na.rm = TRUE),
    bb_asp           = diff(range(latitude, na.rm = TRUE)) / diff(range(longitude, na.rm = TRUE)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(reach_bbox_deg2))

top_10_reach <- family_reach |>
  slice_head(n = 10)




Visualization

# Create world map
world_map <- 
  ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(
    data = map_data("world"), 
    aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),
    fill = "gray90", color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) + 
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) + 
  coord_map(
    projection = "mercator",
    xlim = c(-180, 180),
    ylim = c(-50, 70)
  ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    panel.grid   = element_blank(),
    panel.border = element_blank(),
    axis.text    = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks   = element_blank()
  )

world_map + 
  geom_rect(
    data = top_10_reach,
    aes(xmin  = min_lon, xmax = max_lon, 
        ymin  = min_lat, ymax = max_lat,
        color = family, 
        fill  = after_scale(alpha(color, 0.3))),
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "darkgray", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted") + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "darkgray", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted") + 
  facet_wrap(~ family, ncol = 2) + 
  labs(
    title    = "Global Reach of Top 10 Language Families",
    subtitle = "Total Area of Recorded Speakers",
    caption  = "Source: 'The Languages of the World' TidyTuesday dataset, curated from Glottolog 5.2.1",
    color    = "Family"
       ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title      = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 15),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    panel.spacing   = unit(1, "cm"),
    strip.text      = element_text_repel(hjust = 0.5, size = 11)
  )









ggplot(top_10_reach, aes(x = reorder(family, reach_km), y = reach_km)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#3d1a8e") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Global Reach of Top 10 Language Families",
    subtitle = "By Longest Distance (km) Between Recorded Speakers",
    caption  = "Source: 'The Languages of the World' TidyTuesday dataset, curated from Glottolog 5.2.1",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Haversine Line Distance (km)"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
  )










Analysis & Discussion
It’s unconventional, and maybe crude, to use a rectangular area (calculated from extremes) to measure the global reach of a language family.

Challenges & Solutions
I think the biggest challenge with this visualization is semantic: language family designations don’t really mean much to us. While most Americans might guess that Indo-European is ‘what we speak’, I don’t think languages’ families are common knowledge.

title: INFO 526 Final Project Proposal
text:
Footnotes


Global reach = Geographic area, measured in latitude and longitude, covered by speakers of the language↩︎

title: The Languages of the World
text: Recorded Presentation
